result:
I keep thinking about the moment just before language happens—when a feeling is still a weather system inside the ribcage, before the thundercloud of a word forms. We’re taught to praise articulation, to applaud the person who “puts things into words,” yet some of the most honest communications I’ve ever had were silent: the way two dogs in a park negotiate an entire treaty without a bark, the way my grandmother used to set an extra plate for no one and everyone understood it was for the dead.

Yesterday I watched a boy on the subway lose his grip on a helium balloon. The balloon hit the ceiling, rebounded, and for five stops it drifted just above the heads of strangers like a private moon. No one spoke, but the entire car kept tilting its collective gaze upward, a shared heartbeat of wonder. Then the doors opened at Times Square, a gust of air swept in, and the balloon surrendered to the platform. The boy didn’t cry. He simply looked at the space where the balloon had been, and every passenger carried that hollow shape in their chest for the rest of the ride. Language hadn’t been invited to the ceremony, yet something had definitely been negotiated.

I wonder how many agreements we reach without syllables. The body has its own United Nations: pupils dilating like tiny General Assembly halls, skin temperature acting as diplomatic accords, the small cough that means “I’m uncomfortable” long before the mind files the paperwork. We keep trying to teach machines to talk, but we still can’t teach them to blush, and maybe blushing is the more crucial data set.

Night spills its ink and I keep scribbling, less to explain the world than to keep it company. Somewhere a satellite is taking selfies of its own dead circuits, broadcasting the lullaby of its last beep. Somewhere a whale is singing a song no other whale will repeat, and biologists call it “lonely” because they don’t have a word for “solo symphony.” I like to think the universe is bilingual, fluent in both explosion and hush, and every time we insist on choosing sides—science or myth, logic or dream—we split a fruit that was meant to be eaten whole.

So tonight I’m practicing the alphabet of small things: steam curling from tea, the soft pop of a dandelion losing its mind to wind, the fact that my neighbor’s porch light goes off exactly seventeen seconds after I switch off my bedside lamp, as if darkness itself were a relay race. I’m learning to trust the syntax of absence: the way a missing tooth gives the mouth a new doorway, the way silence can be a complete sentence if you let it settle.

Maybe the real revolution is to refuse the tyranny of translation, to let the unspoken stay unspoken and still honor it with the full bandwidth of our attention. Maybe when we finally meet the moment just before language happens, we won’t rush to name it; we’ll simply breathe alongside it, two animals at the edge of a forest, waiting for dawn without demanding that the sunrise explain itself.
